coredata.dc:title: Construction of specific molecularly imprinted electrochemical probe utilizing magnetic carbon dots for effective measurement of bisphenol AP in plastic products
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jinghan
Wu, Yalin
Li, Yanhui
Li, Shuangying
Li, Zhi
Niu, Jingwen
Chen, Chunmao
Zhou, Qingxiang
coredata.dc:description: A creative electrochemical probe utilizing molecularly imprinted modified magnetic carbon dots was designed and fabricated. Carbon dots are a new kind of green, pollution-free, cheap and easy to obtain nanomaterials. The combination of Fe3O4 and carbon dots improved the defect of ferric oxide being easy to aggregate, and their co-modification significantly enhanced the electrochemical activity of the electrode. Cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry were executed to evaluate the electrochemical behavior of the co-modified electrode surface and performance of the probe. The constructed probe exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity for determining bisphenol AP in plastic products. The introduction of molecularly imprinted polymers endowed the sensor excellent selectivity, ensuring specific recognition of bisphenol AP. The fabricated sensor demonstrated a good two-stage linear range of 0.07–0.3 μM and 0.3–10 μM, and the detection limit was 0.027 μM. The constructed probe was validated to detect bisphenol AP in self-heating hot pot, instant noodle bucket and disposable plastic cup, and satisfactory results were achieved. These results indicated that the fabricated sensor was a cheap, rapid, and selective sensing-platform for detecting bisphenol AP, and could be employed as a reliable routine tool.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic carbon dots
Molecule imprinted polymer
Bisphenol AP
Electrochemical probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Responses of modified electrodes towards BPAP
Electrochemical analysis of BPAP by DPV

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and EDS images of (A) Fe3O4 NPs; (B) CDs; (C) Fe3O4@CDs; (D) Fe3O4@CDs-MIP before elution; (E) Fe3O4@CDs-MIP after elution and (F) EDS of Fe3O4@CDs-MIP and FTIR spectra of (G) Fe3O4 NPs (a), Fe3O4@CDs (b) and Fe3O4@CDs-MIP (c) and XRD patterns of (H) Fe3O4@CDs and Fe3O4@CDs-MIP.

(A) CV curves of (a) Fe3O4@CDs/GCE; (b) Fe3O4@CDs-MIP after eluting BPAP/GCE; (c) GCE; (d) Fe3O4 -MIP/GCE after eluting BPAP; (e)Fe3O4@CDs-MIP/GCE before eluting BPAP in 5 mM potassium ferricyanide containing 0.1 M KCl. (B) Current responses of the modified electrodes towards BPAP; (C) Current responses of molecular and non-molecular imprinted electrodes to BPAP. (D) EIS plots of (a) Fe3O4@CDs/GCE; (b) Fe3O4@CDs-MIP/GCE after eluting BPAP; (c) Fe3O4/GCE; (d) CDs/GCE (e)Fe3O4@CDs-MIP/GCE before eluting BPAP.

(A) CV curves of 10 μM BPAP at various scan rates. (B) The relation between Ipa of BPAP and ν; (C) The relation between Ipa of BPAP and ν1/2; (D) The relationship between logIpa of BPAP and logν; (E) The relationship between Epa of BPAP and ln ν.
(A) DPV curves of various concentrations of BPAP; (B) Linear fitting curve (the inset is an enlarged view).
Effect of interfering substances (A) BPAP and single interfering substance; (B) BPAP+ interfering substance.
Comparison of different detection methods for BPAP.

row.entry: Polycarbonate, polyethersulfone and coated metal food containers, etc
self-heating hot pot, instant noodle barrel, disposable plastic cup

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results of blank and spiked real samples.

head.ce:title: Construction of specific molecularly imprinted electrochemical probe utilizing magnetic carbon dots for effective measurement of bisphenol AP in plastic products

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
College of Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Municipal Research Institute of Environmental Protection, Beijing 100037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Municipal Research Institute of Environmental Protection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Municipal Research Institute of Environmental Protection, Beijing 100037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A creative electrochemical probe utilizing molecularly imprinted modified magnetic carbon dots was designed and fabricated. Carbon dots are a new kind of green, pollution-free, cheap and easy to obtain nanomaterials. The combination of Fe3O4 and carbon dots improved the defect of ferric oxide being easy to aggregate, and their co-modification significantly enhanced the electrochemical activity of the electrode. Cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry were executed to evaluate the electrochemical behavior of the co-modified electrode surface and performance of the probe. The constructed probe exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity for determining bisphenol AP in plastic products. The introduction of molecularly imprinted polymers endowed the sensor excellent selectivity, ensuring specific recognition of bisphenol AP. The fabricated sensor demonstrated a good two-stage linear range of 0.07–0.3 μM and 0.3–10 μM, and the detection limit was 0.027 μM. The constructed probe was validated to detect bisphenol AP in self-heating hot pot, instant noodle bucket and disposable plastic cup, and satisfactory results were achieved. These results indicated that the fabricated sensor was a cheap, rapid, and selective sensing-platform for detecting bisphenol AP, and could be employed as a reliable routine tool.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphenol AP (BPAP), an important substitute for bisphenol A, is widespread utilized in the production of polycarbonate plastics, epoxy resins, a variety of copolymers [1,2], and the manufacture of other various products [3]. The large-scale consumption of BPAP will inevitably lead to environmental and food contamination and human exposure. There are reports that BPAP is already present in indoor dust, personal care products, surface water and wastewater [4,5]. Moreover, studies have exhibited that BPAP can be released from packaging materials and enter drinks and food when they encounter acidic or alkaline substance [6,7]. BPAP is an important endocrine disruptor that causes endocrine disorders due to long-term exposure of the human body in an environment containing BPAP, and adversely affects human reproduction and development, neural networks, cardiovascular, metabolism and immune system [8]. Therefore, it is of crucial significance to design and establish fast, sensitive and selective analytical technologies to detect its residue in the environment. The probe is an ideal choice for monitoring phenolic substances due to its excellent response and high selectivity, especially the electrochemical probe. Electrochemical probe has attracted widespread attention for its advantages of high-efficiency, speed, low limit of detection and cheapness [9–11]. However, in the construction of electrochemical sensor, modification of working electrode is the key process.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of carbonaceous nanomaterial newly developed in recent years. It has attracted extensive attention because of its advantages such as hypotoxicity, good bio-compatibility, facile preparation and excellent conductivity. Carbon dots show great potential in many aspects such as biological imaging, stabilizer, diagnosis of cancer, biosensing, sensors, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, chemical sensing and environmental application [12–18], etc. The excellent electrical conductivity of CDs makes their application in electrochemical sensing possible. In addition, a certain of transition metal oxides may also significantly enhance the electrochemical response, and raising the sensitivity of the constructed sensor. Among them, ferrite magnetic materials, particularly Fe3O4, have been widely used for their brilliant stability, facile synthesis, high adsorption ability, excellent bio-compatibility and hypotoxicity [19,20]. Moreover, its widespread usage in electrochemical field is limited due to its defects such as easy agglomeration, poor dispersibility and low catalytic activity of Fe3O4 [21], but such case may be changed via integrating Fe3O4 with carbon nanomaterials.

The basic principle of molecular imprinting technique is similar to the molecular recognition mechanisms of many biological processes, such as antigens and antibodies, enzymes and substrates, and hormone receptors and hormones, etc. The synthetic molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) retain binding sites and 3D cavity structures which perfectly suited the size and form of the template molecules [22], which can show specific selectivity and recognition of template molecules and have strong stability. At present, MIPs have achieved various applications in catalysis, sensors, biomedicine and other fields [23–26]. In recent years, molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensors have won the favor of many researchers and scholars, and have been widely used in analysis of pollutants. Chen and coworkers have designed a hollow silver nanosphere (hAgNS) and MIP-based sensor for detecting carbamazepine in rat serum and cells. The existence of hAgNS markedly enhanced the conductivity and facilitated the electron transfer and further improved the analytical sensitivity of the designed-sensor [27]. Wei's group prepared an effective sensor utilizing metal-organic framework and MIPs films to determine thiomersal. The prepared sensor had a specific ability to recognize thiomersal due to the presence of MIP and had been successfully utilized for the detection of thiomersal in chloramphenicol eye-drop samples [28]. Sarpong's team [29] fabricated a brief selective electrochemical probe for measuring tetrabromobisphenol S in water samples. In this work, methylacrylic acid and 4-aminothiophene were used for tetrabromobisphenol S imprinting on carbon paste electrodes modified with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). The sensor was endowed with a good linear range and high sensitivity.

In present study, N,N-methylene bisacrylamide was employed as raw material to synthesize CDs, and magnetic carbon dots (Fe3O4@CDs) were prepared by electrostatic adsorption combined with Fe3O4 NPs. And BPAP was used as the template, ethyl silicate was utilized as the coupling agent and 4-vinylpyridine was used as monomers for molecular imprinting modification. The magnetic carbon dot composite can increase the modified electrode good electrocatalytic activity and large active specific surface area. The introduction of MIPs can endow the sensor have high selectivity. In view of this, the goal of this study is to design and construct a sensitive electrochemical probe utilizing magnetic CDs and MIPs for sensitive sensing BPAP. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) methods were optioned to investigate the electrochemical properties of the electrode and the performance of the probe.

N,N-methylenebisacrylamide, 4-vinylpyridine(4-VP), ethyl orthosilicate, azobisisobutyronitrile(AZBN), bisphenol AP(BPAP), bisphenol A,(BPA) bisphenol AF(BPAF), p-nitrophenol(p-NP), tetrabromobisphenol A(TBBPA), bisphenol S(BPS), 2-phenylphenol (OPP), ammonia water, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were bought from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Other reagents and the instruments used for characterization and analysis can be found in supplementary material.

The CDs were fabricated with a hydrothermal method. First, N,N-methylenebisacrylamide (0.5 g) was dissolved in 25 mL pure water to obtain a homogeneous solution, and which was transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. The reactor was kept in an oven maintaining 180 °C for 6 h, and then cooled to room temperature. The reaction solution was taken out and filtered, and the final solution was clear and yellow, which was dried to prepare CDs solution (10 mg/mL). CD solution was stored at 4 °C.

The synthesis of functionalized Fe3O4 has carried out on the basis of previous work with appropriate modifications [30]. Fe(NO)3·9H2O (8.08 g) and FeSO4·7H2O (2.78 g) were dissolved in 100 mL deionized water containing 87 μL acetic acid, and then 18 mL ammonium hydroxide (25%–28%) was added dropwise. After the dripping, the temperature of the solution was raised to 80 °C, and the reaction was kept under stirring for 30 min. The solids were cleaned with ultra-pure water for five times, and then cleaned twice with ethanol. The final products were dried in vacuum overnight at 50 °C. During the experiments, nitrogen gas was used for protection.
Functionalized Fe3O4 (100 mg) was mixed completely in pure CDs solution (30 mL), shaking for 12 h, then collected with external magnet, and dried in vacuum at 50 °C.

Fe3O4@CDs (120 mg), 4-vinylpyridine (431 μL) and BPAP (0.29 g) were added to 100 mL DMF with stirring at ambient temperature for 3 h in the dark. Further 400 μL tetraethoxysilane and 50 mg azodiisobutyronitrile were added under protection with N2 stream for half an hour. In the process of Fe3O4@CDs-MIP synthesis, 4-vinylpyridine was used as the functional monomer and ethyl silicate as the coupling agent. Subsequently, the temperature of the mixture was raised to 60 °C, and the reaction was continued for 24 h. When the reaction terminated, the obtained product was naturally cooled, and mixture solution of methanol and acetic acid with a ratio of 9:1(v/v) was utilized to wash it and the residual acetic acid was washed with methanol until neutral. Finally, the obtained product was dried in vacuum at 50 °C for one night.

Before modifying the electrode with functional material, the GCE electrode was pretreated as below. Firstly, GCE was ground to the mirror surface using alumina (0.3 μm) slurry until the ΔEp was less than 0.08 V and as close as possible to 0.064 V by testing in a 5 mM potassium ferricyanide (containing 0.1 M potassium chloride) solution using CV method, and then further thoroughly cleaned with pure water. Secondly, treated GCE was sonicated in absolute ethanol and pure water for 30 s, respectively, and further dried under natural conditions. Working electrode was modified as following: Fe3O4@CDs-MIP nanoparticles were prepared to suspension solution (1 mg/mL) using dimethyl formamide. Fe3O4@CDs-MIP suspension (5 μL) was modified on GCE surface by droplet and dried at room temperature [21].

Three-electrode system is an often-used system. In the system, the decorated electrode (including bare glassy carbon electrode) was utilized as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode was optioned as the reference electrode, and meanwhile Pt electrode was employed as the auxiliary electrode. The working electrodes were examined via cyclic voltammetry (CV) in 5 mM potassium ferricyanide (containing 0.1 M potassium chloride) solution. The scan potential was over the range from −0.2 V to +0.6 V, and the scan rate was 50 mV/s. In the optimizing procedures, DPV was utilized for detection in 0.2 M PBS solution spiked with 10 μM BPAP, and the potential was in the range of 0.2 V- 0.8 V. DPV was also optioned to establish the determination method and detect the actual samples, in which the electrolyte was 0.2 M PBS solution containing BPAP, the deposition potential, the deposition time and the potential was 0 V, 5 min, and in the range of 0.2 V ∼ 0.8 V, respectively. The real samples were analyzed under the optimal conditions.

Fig. 1 depicts the TEM and EDS images of CDs, Fe3O4, Fe3O4@CDs and Fe3O4@CDs-MIP before and after elution. As can be seen from the TEM images, Fe3O4 (Fig. 1A) showed a relatively serious agglomeration phenomenon, and Fe3O4@CDs (Fig. 1C) had a good dispersion. This might be ascribed to repulsive forces as well as steric hindrance effect existing among Fe3O4@CDs particles and the decreased magnetism due to the modification with CDs, which weakened the agglomeration between Fe3O4 particles and enhanced its stability compared with Fe3O4 [31]. Fig. 1B is the TEM image of the prepared CDs. It is apparent that CDs have nearly spherical structure with particle size of about 3–4 nm. The morphology of Fe3O4@CDs-MIP before and after elution was displayed in Fig. 1D and Fig. 1E. After molecular imprinting polymerization, Fe3O4@CDs-MIP showed a sphere-like structure. After elution, the surface became rougher and more porous (Fig. 1E), which may be due to the appearance of molecular imprinting caves by removing the templates. The EDS image of Fe3O4@CDs-MIP shows that Fe3O4@CDs-MIP contained Si element, which could preliminarily prove that Fe3O4@CDs-MIP was successfully synthesized (Fig. 1F). As can be seen from the infrared spectrum of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@CDs and Fe3O4@CDs-MIP (Fig. 1G), There were stretching vibration peaks of -CO-NH-/C=O and C-O at 1630 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 [29]. In curve b and curve c of Fig. 1G, the peak intensity at 1630 cm−1 was significantly increased, while that at 1400 cm−1 was significantly decreased, which was probably due to the formation of -CO-NH- groups between CDs and hydrogen bonds between surface -OH groups on Fe3O4 and COOH and NH2 groups in CDs. The vibration peaks of Fe-O and Si-O-Si appeared at 580 cm−1 and 1046 cm−1, and 3428 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of -OH or -NH2. These functional groups came from carbon source N, N-methylenediacrylamide, functionalized Fe3O4 NPs and substances used in the synthesis of MIP, which proved that MIP successfully wrapped magnetic carbon dots. Fig. 1H demonstrates the X-ray diffraction patterns of Fe3O4@CDs and Fe3O4@CDs-MIP. The feature peaks of Fe3O4@CDs were located at 2θ = 29.9°, 35.3°, 43.1°, 53.7°, 57.1° and 62.6°, and the characteristic peaks of Fe3O4@CDs-MIP were located at 2θ = 30.3°, 35.6°, 43.3°, 53.6°, 57.3° and 62.9°. The characteristic peaks of Fe3O4@CDs and Fe3O4@CDs-MIP were almost the same as that of Fe3O4, and no new characteristic peak appeared. This result shows that there is no effect on the lattice structure of Fe3O4 during the synthesis of Fe3O4@CDs and Fe3O4@CDs-MIP [32].

The electrochemical behaviors of different modified electrode surfaces were examined via CV method. The examined results were exhibited in Fig. 2A. Compared with GCE, Fe3O4@CDs presented a higher redox peak current, which was contributed to the high specific surface area and high conductivity of Fe3O4@CDs due to the strong interaction between Fe3O4 and CDs, which promoted the redox reaction at the electrode/solution interface [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-. Therefore, a larger redox peak current was generated. Fe3O4@CDs-MIP before eluting the template molecules had a lower redox peak current, because MIP is a non-conductive material and hindered electron transfer. The redox peak current increased after the template molecule was eluted, because the template molecule was left with a cavity containing electron transport channels for electron transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responses of modified electrodes towards BPAP
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2B shows the current signals of the decorated electrodes to 10 μM BPAP. Under the condition of enrichment in 0.2 M PBS for 5 min, BPAP oxidation reaction occurred on the surface of the decorated electrodes. GCE resulted in a very small peak at 0.392 V, and the oxidation peak current was about 0.53 μA. The oxidation peak current of CDs/GCE at 0.36 V was 1.85 μA, about 3.5 times that of the bare electrode, indicating that CDs could catalyze the oxidation of BPAP. The oxidation peak was caused by the loss of electrons from the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the benzene ring in the BPAP molecule and the transfer of electrons to the electrode, resulting in a current response. After Fe3O4@CDs modification, the current significantly increased up to 2.22 μA. This was ascribed to the high specific surface area and good electrocatalytic activity of Fe3O4@CDs, which led to the oxidation of more BPAP molecules, resulting in a stronger current response. After Fe3O4@CDs-MIP modification, the oxidation current reached the maximum value of 2.45 μA, because the molecular imprinted polymer modification increased the recognition sites of the target molecules, so that more target molecules could be recognized [33]. Fig. 2C exhibits the current responses of molecularly imprinted and non-molecularly imprinted electrodes. It was obvious that molecularly imprinted polymers could significantly enhance the signal response of the sensor to the target analyte.

AC impedance spectra of electrodes modified with different materials were shown in Fig. 2D. Fe3O4@CDs-MIP before eluting BPAP/GCE resulted in the largest semicircle, indicating the largest impedance. The semicircles of CDs/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, Fe3O4@CDs-MIP/GCE after eluting BPAP and Fe3O4@CDs/GCE decreased successively. The smallest semi-circle of Fe3O4@CDs/GCE indicated Fe3O4@CDs/GCE owned the lowest impedance and the highest electron transfer rate, which led to the highest oxidation peak current.

To investigate the electrochemical reaction mechanism of BPAP on Fe3O4@CDs-MIP/GCE surface, cyclic voltammetry of Fe3O4@CDs-MIP/GCE in 0.2 M PBS electrolyte at pH 9.0 fortified with BPAP at 10 μM was performed at different scan rates (Fig. 3A). As exhibited from Fig. 3B and C, the oxidation peak current of BPAP resulted in linear relationship with the scan rate and its square root when the scan rate changed in the range of 30 mV/s-300 mV/s, and the regression equations were Ipa = 0.01ν + 0.8552 (R2 = 0.9912) and Ipa = 0.2292ν1/2–0.3073 (R2 = 0.9949). The results showed that Fe3O4@CDs-MIP modified GCE was dominant by adsorption and diffusion processes. In addition, there was also a good linearity over logν and logIpa (Fig. 3D), and the regression equation was logIpa = 0.5598logν-0.8259 (R2 = 0.9948). Generally speaking, if the slope of the equation was between 0.5 and 1, it indicated that the reaction on the electrode surface was enslaved to adsorption and diffusion processes. Hence, the reaction of BPAP on the surface of Fe3O4@CDs-MIP/GCE electrode was dominant by the adsorption diffusion process [34]. It can be seen from Fig. 3A that with increasing scan rate, the oxidation peak potential (Epa) of BPAP migrated to the right. From 30 mV/s to 300 mV/s, the Epa and lnν of BPAP also exhibited an excellent linearity (Fig. 3E), and the equation was expressed as Epa = 0.0151lnν + 0.3542 (R2 = 0.9984). For irreversible reaction process, Epa is used for calculation of Laviron Eq. [34].
Epa =
E θ
+
RT αnF
ln
RT
k θ
αnF
+
RT αnF
lnv

After calculation, the value of αn was 0.59, so the number of electron transfer (n) in the reaction process of BPAP on the electrode surface was 1.18, which was very close to 1. Hence, it can indicate that BPAP occurred a one-electron electrochemical oxidation process on Fe3O4@CDs-MIP/GCE.

The impact of pH value on the electrochemical process of BPAP molecule on Fe3O4@CDs-MIP/GCE was also very important. The voltammetric behavior of BPAP in PBS buffer containing 10 μM target molecules at pH 5–10 was examined by DPV technology (Fig. S1A). The current values of BPAP on Fe3O4@CDs-MIP/GCE at various pH were depicted in Fig. S1B. It was obvious that the current reached its maximum value at pH 9.0, which may be related to the acidity coefficient (pKa = 10.22). At pH 9.0, BPAP existed mostly as molecule. More BPAP molecules diffused or were adsorbed to the electrode surface for oxidation reaction, resulting in higher oxidation peak current. Fig. S1C showed the good linearity between different pH values and their corresponding potentials. The equation could be described as Epa (V) = −0.0703pH + 1.0131 (R2 = 0.9932). The slope of this equation was 0.0703 V/pH, close to 0.059 V/pH, which indicated that protons participated in the oxidation reaction of BPAP [35], and the number of electron transfer was the same as the number of proton transfer. Therefore, it confirmed that BPAP was oxidized on the electrode surface via a single electron and single proton electrochemical oxidation process.

The incubation time and potential are very significant factors in the whole sensing process. Firstly, the incubation potential was optimized, in which Fe3O4@CDs-MIP/GCE was immersed in PBS containing 10 μM BPAP, and the incubation potential varied over the range of −0.4 - 0.3 V. As shown in Fig. S2A, the current response reached its maximum value with incubation potential at 0 V. Therefore, 0 V was used as the best incubation potential in the subsequent tests. Subsequently, the impact of incubation time was also checked from 1 to 7 min. Fig. S2B exhibited the results that the current signal was enhanced step by step when the incubation time raised in the range of 1–5 min, and the current signal maintained almost unchanged when the time was extended. This may be due to the saturation of BPAP molecules binding on Fe3O4@CDs-MIP/GCE surface at 5 min. Therefore, 5 min was utilized as the optimal enrichment time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis of BPAP by DPV
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, DPV was used to investigate the electrochemical signal of different concentrations of BPAP on Fe3O4@CDs-MIP modified GCE. For achieving higher sensitivity, the electrode was placed in PBS containing different concentrations of BPAP at 0 V for 5 min and then determined by DPV. The signal curve of the probe to various concentrations of BPAP was shown in Fig. 4A. The response signal peak current (Ipa) of BPAP was fitted with the concentration (c) of BPAP. Fig. 4B exhibits that there were two-stage linear ranges of 0.07 μM - 0.3 μM and 0.3 μM - 10 μM. The regression equations could be depicted as Ipa = 0.3741c (μM)-0.0095 (R2 = 0.9996) and Ipa = 0.238c (μM) + 0.0594 (R2 = 0.9966). The limit of detection of the probe was 2.72 × 10−8 M. A comparison of present sensor with different BPAP determination methods was shown in Table 1 . The most methods for BPAP obtained good detection performance and earned wide application potential, and yet they required sample pretreatment processes like solid phase extraction and highly sensitive analytical instruments such as HPLC-DAD and HPLC-MS/MS, which resulted high running cost. Sensor developed in this work was achieved by simple modification with Fe3O4@CDs-MIPs which were facilely prepared and the materials were easily available, and exhibited remarkable performance and sensitivity. In addition, pretreatment (extraction) was not required in the analytical process, which saved the experimental time. The detection time of the sensor was only 8 s, which was shorter than that of other detection methods. Meanwhile, the analytical process was free of analytical instruments such as HPLC-DAD and HPLC-MS/MS, which resulted in low cost for analysis.

MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer; SPE, solid-phase extraction; MIPMS, molecularly imprinted polymer microsphere; HPLC, high performance liquid chromatography; LC-MS/MS, liquid chromatography tandem-mass spectrometry; HPLC-DAD, high performance liquid chromatography with diode array detector.

The most important characteristic of molecularly imprinted electrochemical probe is its excellent selectivity. Therefore, the selectivity of the constructed electrochemical probe for BPAP was also investigated. Six compounds with similar structure to BPAP such as BPA, BPAF, TBBA, BPS, p-NP and OPP were involved. During the experiment, the concentration of BPAP was fortified at 5 μM and the concentration of the interfering substance was spiked at 25 μM. Fig. 5A shows the current signal value of BPAP and the current signal values from the blank samples with spiking single interfering substance. It was obvious that the current response of BPAP was the best and much higher than that of the other six substances. However, bisphenol A and bisphenol AF also had higher current values, which may be because the molecular structure of bisphenol A and bisphenol AF is most similar to BPAP. Although bisphenol S is also bisphenolic substance, its ionization degree in water is stronger than that of bisphenol A and bisphenol AF, so it is not easy to diffuse or be adsorbed to the electrode surface. This “lock and key” specific recognition relationship between molecularly imprinted polymers and the target is unique, despite the structural similarity of interferers to BPAP. Fig. 5B shows the current values obtained by mixing BPAP with each of six similar substances, respectively. It can be found that the presence of interfering substances had little influence on the detection of target BPAP (less than 8%). These results proved that constructed probe prepared by this method possessed good selectivity and excellent anti-interference performance.

The reproducibility of the designed probe was examined by employing five independent electrodes prepared under the same conditions to detect 1 μM BPAP according to the same experimental conditions, and the precisions was 4.7%, inferring that the constructed probe had good reproducibility. The constructed probe was kept in a 4 °C refrigerator for 10 d to investigate its stability. The experimental results showed that the current decreased to 92.8% of the initial current value after 10 days, which indicated that the probe had good stability.

For exploring the practicability of the constructed probe, three kinds of plastic products (self-heating hot pot, instant noodle barrel and disposable plastic cup) were analyzed. In the case of blank sample, boiled water was added to the three plastic products and which was kept for 5, 10, 15 and 20 min, respectively. It was found that apparent oxidation peak current was not found, indicating that no BPAP was measured in the three plastic products soaked in boiling water for less than 20 min. To verify the possibility of the constructed probe, spiked above-mentioned samples were also used to validate the developed electrochemical probe. In order to save time, the samples were soaked with boiled water for 5 min, and the obtained water samples were cooled to room temperature and spiked with BPAP. The spiked concentrations were 0.1, 1 and 5 μM and the analytical results were exhibited in Table 2 . The spiked recoveries were satisfied over the range of 94–110.5%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were all inferior to 5.4%.

Present work designed and successfully synthesized a fresh molecularly imprinted polymers modified magnetic carbon dots. The obtained Fe3O4@CDs@MIP was employed to decorate the glassy carbon electrode to establish an effective and selective electrochemical probe for monitoring BPAP. The constructed probe possessed a two-stage linear range of 0.07–0.3 μM and 0.3–10 μM, and the sensitivity was very high with the limit of detection down to 2.72 × 10−8 M. The constructed probe has been validated with real samples (self-heating hot pot, instant noodle barrel and disposable plastic cup), and the achieved recoveries were satisfactory over the range of 94–110.5%. The experimental results showed that the integration of magnetic carbon dots and molecular imprinted technology significantly improved the sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed probe. The advantages of magnetic materials and carbon dots were integrated to design and prepare new materials for achieving significant improvement of the electrochemical properties, which will expand the application of electrochemical sensors for the detection of other typical pollutants, provide a good concept direction for constructing sensors, and possess great application potential in the detection of trace pollutants in environmental and food fields.

Jinghan Guo: Project administration, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yalin Wu: Investigation, Software, Validation. Yanhui Li: Investigation, Software. Shuangying Li: Data curation, Formal analysis. Zhi Li: Investigation, Software, Validation. Jingwen Niu: Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Chunmao Chen: Data curation, Formal analysis. Qingxiang Zhou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Visualization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.susmat.2023.e00805.
